stylePrompt: Create a completely original lighting product inspired by the spirit of Mid-Century Modern design from approximately 1945 to 1970. The design should feel as if it could have been created by an unknown MCM master designer, but reinterpreted for contemporary premium interiors. Do not copy any existing famous lamp. Avoid directly reproducing Sputnik chandeliers, George Nelson Bubble Lamps, Noguchi Akari lamps, Arco-style floor lamps, PH lamps, Serge Mouille lamps, or any other recognizable historical lighting product. The design should express clean geometry, functional beauty, optimistic futurism, sculptural simplicity, balanced asymmetry, warm material honesty, human-scale proportions, elegant engineering, mass-production logic, and strong silhouette recognition. Draw selectively from the configured inspiration sources, specific elements, materials, and colors. Do not combine all available references in one design; use only a small, coherent subset so each result can explore a different branch of Mid-Century Modern design.
specificElements: The angled fin structure of a 1950s concept car
The offset balance of an Alexander Calder mobile
The circular tuning dial of a vintage radio
The tapered wooden leg geometry of an Eames chair
The aerodynamic nose cone of a Jet Age aircraft
The ribbed grille pattern of a vintage speaker
The suspended orbital rhythm of an atomic model
The cantilevered roofline of a Case Study House
The tension line of a suspension bridge cable
The sculptural void of a Noguchi coffee table
The layered shadow rhythm of matte black acoustic louvers
A warm amber halo reflected across walnut furniture
A discreet dark power cable integrated as an intentional functional line
A restrained horizontal rib rhythm applied to one functional surface
A slender splayed support geometry adapted to the selected lighting type
A controlled contrast between one luminous surface and one dark structural component
avoid: Literal copies of ribbed beehive table lamps
metalTranslationPrompt: Use authentic Mid-Century Modern metal language: tapered cone or dome shades, slim tubular arms, compact spherical joints, warm brushed brass, aged bronze, blackened steel, and walnut-toned powder coating expressed as metal finish rather than wood.
